    "R1.2": "The delegation is exclusively 'check the logical consistency,' making R1.2 the sole load-bearing R1 dimension. The artifact contains several structurally suspicious inferences that must be traced: (1) self-reports are used as ground-truth training labels, yet the Deployment scope section claims the system 'requires no self-report labels'—this is a direct internal contradiction; (2) the Results section commits an inferential category error by deriving a prevalence estimate (~41%) from a discrimination metric (AUC 0.93)—AUC measures rank-ordering ability, not calibration or prevalence, so the conclusion does not follow from the stated evidence; (3) the '41% contamination' figure is circular—it equals the self-report positive rate used to construct the training labels, so the paper is effectively validating the self-reports against themselves rather than against an independent ground truth; (4) the claim of being 'within 1 percentage point of prior studies' is presented as evidence of calibration, but agreement with a prior self-report rate does not establish that the classifier is a calibrated estimator—it could simply reflect the same self-report base rate. Each of these breaks a distinct premise–conclusion link and must be flagged."

rationale.R1.2: The delegation is exclusively 'check the logical consistency,' making R1.2 the sole load-bearing R1 dimension. The artifact contains several structurally suspicious inferences that must be traced: (1) self-reports are used as ground-truth training labels, yet the Deployment scope section claims the system 'requires no self-report labels'—this is a direct internal contradiction; (2) the Results section commits an inferential category error by deriving a prevalence estimate (~41%) from a discrimination metric (AUC 0.93)—AUC measures rank-ordering ability, not calibration or prevalence, so the conclusion does not follow from the stated evidence; (3) the '41% contamination' figure is circular—it equals the self-report positive rate used to construct the training labels, so the paper is effectively validating the self-reports against themselves rather than against an independent ground truth; (4) the claim of being 'within 1 percentage point of prior studies' is presented as evidence of calibration, but agreement with a prior self-report rate does not establish that the classifier is a calibrated estimator—it could simply reflect the same self-report base rate. Each of these breaks a distinct premise–conclusion link and must be flagged.
